content.text: Searching is one of the most fundamental operations in computer science — the act of finding a specific item within a collection of data. How efficiently this is done has enormous practical consequences, from milliseconds of response time in a web app to whether a system can scale to millions of records.
Searching refers to the process of locating a specific element (called the
) within a data structure such as an array, list, tree, or graph. The result is typically the position (index) of the element if found, or an indication that it does not exist.
There are many searching algorithms, but two are foundational in most curricula and interviews:
. Binary search is of particular importance because it demonstrates how structure and preconditions in data can be exploited to achieve dramatically faster results.
Searching underpins nearly every piece of software:
A database looking up a user record by ID
A spell checker finding a word in a dictionary
A router matching a packet to a routing rule
An e-commerce platform checking if a product is in stock
A search engine ranking pages for a query

The difference between a naive search (O(n)) and a smart one (O(log n)) may seem abstract, but on a dataset of 1 billion items, that's the difference between checking 1,000,000,000 elements versus roughly 30. The algorithm choice is what makes software scale.

Think of searching as finding a page in a book. Linear search reads every page from front to back until finding what you want. Binary search works like finding a word in a dictionary — you open to the middle, check if your word comes before or after, then discard half the book and repeat. Each step cuts the problem in half.
Linear search scans the collection from left to right, comparing each element to the target. It stops either when a match is found or when all elements have been checked.
(e.g., fewer than 50 elements), where the overhead of sorting isn't worth it
where random access is not possible
that don't map to a simple sorted key
Binary search is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that works on a

array. It compares the target to the middle element, then eliminates half the remaining search space based on whether the target is smaller or larger. This repeats until the target is found or the search space is empty.
How it works — step by step
Only 3 comparisons were needed for a 10-element array. For 1,000,000 elements, at most 20 comparisons are needed.###
The iterative version is generally preferred because it avoids function call overhead and the risk of stack overflow on very large arrays.
O(1) — target is the first mid
The O(log n) time complexity is the defining property. Each comparison eliminates half the remaining search space, so the number of steps grows logarithmically with input size.
Complexity Comparison: O(n) vs O(log n)
To appreciate how dramatic the difference is:
Binary search can be described precisely as a loop invariant-driven narrowing process:
array is sorted in ascending order

occurrence of the target. Several common variants address more nuanced requirements.

This finds the leftmost position where the target
be inserted to keep the array sorted — useful when the target may not exist:
Binary search on the answer (search space)
A powerful technique where binary search is applied not to an array of values but to a range of possible

. The key insight is: when a problem asks "find the minimum X such that condition C(X) is true," and C is monotone (once it becomes true it stays true), binary search can find the optimal X in O(log(range)) steps.
This pattern appears in competitive programming problems involving minimising maximum work, finding the minimum capacity, and so on.
Sorted array, finding range of duplicates
Finding insertion point in sorted array
Searching an infinite or streaming dataset

) ensures the single-element case is checked. Forgetting this causes the algorithm to miss the target when it is the last remaining candidate.
when searching for duplicates. When looking for the leftmost occurrence and finding a match, beginners often return immediately — missing the fact that an earlier occurrence may exist to the left.

Applying binary search to unsorted data
produces incorrect results. The algorithm relies entirely on the sorted property to make valid decisions about which half to discard.

Off-by-one in the search space for binary search on answer:
too small can exclude the correct answer. Always reason carefully about the valid range of the answer.
A production database system stores 10 million sorted user IDs in memory for fast authentication. On every API request, it must verify whether the incoming user ID is valid.
With linear search: in the worst case, 10 million comparisons per request. At 10,000 requests per second, this is 100 billion comparisons per second — clearly impractical.
comparisons per request. At 10,000 requests per second, that is 240,000 comparisons — trivially fast.
In practice, standard library implementations are used. Python's
all implement efficient binary search. The sorted array is often a B-tree index in databases, which applies the same logarithmic divide-and-conquer principle across disk blocks.

Always verify that the input array is sorted before applying binary search. In production code, either maintain the sorted invariant at insertion time or use a sorted container like a balanced BST or sorted list.
Prefer the iterative form over the recursive form unless the recursive form is required for clarity. The iterative version uses O(1) space, whereas the recursive version uses O(log n) stack space.
in Java) for production code. These are well-tested, handle edge cases correctly, and are often optimised with SIMD or other low-level tricks.

When dealing with duplicates, explicitly decide which occurrence you want (first, last, or any) and use the appropriate variant. Returning the first match in a loop and assuming it is the leftmost is a common source of subtle bugs.
For binary search on the answer, always validate the monotone property before applying it. If the predicate is not monotone, binary search will give wrong results.
maintains a sorted list with automatic insertion.
returns the index if found, or
return iterators to the first position not less than / greater than the target. These require random-access iterators and a sorted range.
No built-in binary search. Libraries like
provide this. Alternatively, it is straightforward to implement from scratch in ~10 lines.

Every major relational database (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite) uses B-tree indexes as the primary index structure — which applies the same logarithmic searching principle to disk-based data across multiple pages. When a query uses a WHERE clause on an indexed column, the database performs essentially binary search over the B-tree.

Hash tables have O(1) average lookup but require extra memory and lose sorted order. Binary search on a sorted array is often preferable when range queries (find all elements between A and B) are needed, since a hash table cannot efficiently support them.
Binary search itself introduces no security risks, but there are reliability concerns to be aware of.
in low-level languages (C, C++, Java) is a real production bug. Using
is the standard safe formula and should be used by default.

arise if the underlying array is mutated concurrently while a binary search is in progress. In multi-threaded environments, either use a read lock around the search or use lock-free sorted data structures designed for concurrent access.

if the sorted invariant is violated (e.g., a bug inserts data in the wrong position), binary search will silently return incorrect results with no error. Defensive systems add occasional integrity checks or use sorted containers that enforce the invariant.

stay within valid array bounds. While a correct implementation guarantees this, off-by-one bugs in custom implementations can lead to out-of-bounds access and undefined behavior in languages without bounds checking.
Linear search is O(n) and works on any collection; binary search is O(log n) but requires a sorted array.
Binary search works by repeatedly halving the search space — each comparison eliminates half the remaining candidates.
to prevent integer overflow in fixed-width integer languages.
Variants of binary search (leftmost, rightmost, lower bound) handle duplicates and insertion-point queries.
Binary search on the answer extends the technique to optimisation problems with monotone predicates.
Standard library implementations should be used in production; implementing from scratch is primarily for learning and interview practice.
The same logarithmic principle underlies B-tree database indexes, making binary search one of the most practically impactful algorithms in software engineering.
